card.title: Why the Sky Is Blue
card.teaser: The sun is yellow-white, space is black. So why is the air above you blue?
card.coverImagePrompt: A dramatic view looking straight up into a deep blue midday sky from a mountain summit, wisps of cirrus cloud, sunlight streaming through thin air, ultra-wide lens

lessons.title: A question that stumped everyone for centuries
lessons.body: Look up on a clear day. Why blue? Not red, not green, not a mix of everything. The answer involves the exact size of molecules in the air, a Victorian physicist named Rayleigh, and the fact that sunlight is secretly a rainbow pretending to be white.

visual.imagePrompt: A person lying in grass looking up at an impossibly blue sky, point-of-view shot, dreamy and soft

lessons.title: Sunlight is a disguise
lessons.body: White sunlight is a mix of every visible color — red, orange, yellow, green, blue, violet. Each color is light of a different wavelength: red is long and stretched, blue and violet are short and tight. When that cocktail enters Earth's atmosphere, it runs into roughly 10^25 air molecules per cubic meter. And different colors react differently.

keyTerms.definition: The distance between peaks of a light wave; red is long, blue is short

visual.imagePrompt: A prism splitting a white beam of sunlight into a spectrum of colors on a dark surface, crisp rainbow

lessons.title: Small wavelengths bounce, big ones cruise
lessons.body: Air molecules are tiny — much smaller than even a wavelength of blue light. When a light wave meets such a molecule, the short-wavelength colors (blue and violet) scatter in every direction far more strongly than the long-wavelength ones (red and orange). This is called Rayleigh scattering, and its strength goes up with the fourth power of frequency.

keyTerms.definition: Scattering of light by particles much smaller than its wavelength; sharply favors shorter wavelengths

visual.imagePrompt: Stylized diagram of blue light waves bouncing off tiny spherical air molecules in every direction, red light passing through straight

lessons.title: So why not violet?
lessons.body: Violet light actually scatters even more strongly than blue. So shouldn't the sky be violet? Two reasons it isn't: first, sunlight contains less violet to begin with. Second, our eyes have three color receptors, and the combination of sensitivities and the atmospheric mix lands firmly on blue. A honeybee, with different receptors, sees a different sky.
lessons.funFact: At sunset, sunlight travels through more atmosphere on the way to your eyes. All the blue scatters away long before it reaches you — leaving red and orange.

visual.imagePrompt: Split-panel illustration: a human eye seeing blue sky on one side, a bee seeing a different sky pattern on the other

lessons.title: Color is a conversation
lessons.body: The sky doesn't 'have' a color the way a wall has paint. The blue you see is a collaboration between sunlight, the size of air molecules, and the receptors in your head. Change any one of them, and the sky changes. Physics isn't abstract — it's the reason your view out the window looks like anything at all.

quiz.question: What physical effect makes the sky blue?
quiz.explanation: Rayleigh scattering by air molecules preferentially deflects short-wavelength light — the blue end of the spectrum.

quiz.question: Which wavelengths scatter the most strongly in Earth's atmosphere?
quiz.explanation: Scattering strength scales with 1/wavelength^4, so short wavelengths scatter dramatically more than long ones.

quiz.question: Why is the sky blue and not violet, given that violet scatters even more?
options: The sun emits less violet, and our eyes are less sensitive to it
Violet is absorbed by ozone entirely
The Earth's magnetic field filters it out
quiz.explanation: Solar output is weaker in violet, and human eyes respond more strongly to blue — so the dominant perceived color is blue.

sources.title: Light and Color in the Outdoors
On the scattering of light by small particles
